title: Watch the Moment When the Wreck of the Titanic Was First Discovered (1985)
description:
in History |   	May 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The wreck of the RMS TitanôÙic has nevôÙer ceased to comôÙmand attenôÙtion, from pop-culôÙturôÙa
body:
in History |   	May 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The wreck of the RMS TitanôÙic has nevôÙer ceased to comôÙmand attenôÙtion, from pop-culôÙturôÙal fasôÙciôÙnaôÙtion to sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic scrutiôÙny and everyôÙthing in between. That can make it seem, espeôÙcialôÙly to the younger genôÙerôÙaôÙtions, as if humanôÙiôÙty has been gazôÙing upon its remains since they first setôÙtled at the botôÙtom of the North Atlantic Ocean. In fact, the preôÙcise locaôÙtion of the shipôÙwreck went unknown for more than 73 years, between the day of the disôÙasôÙter, April 15th, 1912, and that of the disôÙcovôÙery, SepôÙtemôÙber 1, 1985. In the video above, you can watch the very moment debris from theô TitanôÙicô first came into the view of Argo, the unmanned underôÙsea camôÙera used by theô researchers seekôÙing it out.
ãSomeôÙbody should get Bob,ã says one of the crew as soon as it becomes clear, even on their low-resôÙoôÙluôÙtion black-and-white monôÙiôÙtor, that theyãre lookôÙing at man-made objects on the sea floor. And well they should have: the Bob in quesôÙtion is oceanogôÙraôÙphôÙer and Argo invenôÙtor Robert BalôÙlard, whoãd been activeôÙly thinkôÙing about how to find the TitanôÙicô since at least the earôÙly nineôÙteen-sevôÙenôÙties and boardôÙed Woods Hole OceanoôÙgraphôÙic InstiôÙtuteãs R/Vô Knorr with intent to find it.
In truth, the voyôÙage was financed by the U.S. Navy, which had much less interôÙest in findôÙing the wreck of the TitanôÙic than those of the USS ScorôÙpiôÙon andô ThreshôÙer, two nuclear subôÙmarines lost in the sixôÙties. If BalôÙlard could look for them, so the deal went, he could use the expeôÙdiôÙtionãs spare time and resources on his lifeãs misôÙsion.


	

After deterôÙminôÙing that theô ScorôÙpiôÙonô andô ThreshôÙer had implodôÙed, BalôÙlard and the Knorr crew conôÙtinôÙued on to the genôÙerôÙal area in which the TitanôÙic sank.ô KnowôÙing that the infaôÙmousôÙly ãunsinkôÙableã ocean linôÙer would have been subôÙject to the same mighty underôÙsea presôÙsure, they kept their eyes open, through Argo, for simôÙiôÙlarôÙly scatôÙtered fragôÙments rather than intact secôÙtions of the hull.ô As the video shows us, the stratôÙeôÙgy worked: only when a trail of debris leads them to an idenôÙtiôÙfiôÙable boilôÙer, proof posôÙiôÙtive that theyãd found what they were lookôÙing for, does the cheer go up. BalôÙlard would go on to disôÙcovôÙer othôÙer wideôÙly known shipôÙwrecks ã the batôÙtleôÙship BisôÙmarôÙck, the airôÙcraft carôÙriôÙer USS YorkôÙtown in 1998 ã but one susôÙpects that nothôÙing quite matchôÙes that firstô TitanôÙicô high.

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: What Happened to Jesusã Twelve Disciples After the BibleãIt Wasnãt Pretty
description: The stoôÙries in the Bible have been told in many ways, not least through film. Among the many cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic adapôÙtaôÙtions of ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙtyãs
body:
The stoôÙries in the Bible have been told in many ways, not least through film. Among the many cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic adapôÙtaôÙtions of ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙtyãs holy book, none comes to mind that ends with freeze-frame title cards explainôÙing the latôÙer fate of each charôÙacôÙter, in the manôÙner of AniôÙmal House,ô AmerôÙiôÙcan GrafôÙfiôÙti, orô GoodôÙfelôÙlas. This is surôÙprisôÙing, since that device could do much to satôÙisôÙfy our curiosôÙiôÙty about so many secôÙondary BibôÙliôÙcal figôÙures. Take the twelve disôÙciôÙples of Jesus Christ, whose livesô HochelaôÙga creôÙator TomôÙmie Trelawny takes as his subôÙject in the new video above. Be warned: things didôÙnãt end parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly well for most of them.
Peter, who ãhas to be one of the most studôÙied figôÙures in hisôÙtoôÙry,ã seems to have endôÙed his days in Rome. ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙtyãs rapid spread there in the first cenôÙtuôÙry AD, evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly brought about a crackôÙdown by the rulôÙing class. The emperôÙor Nero blamed the fire of 64 on ChrisôÙtians, and Peter, now known as Saint Peter, was among the vicôÙtims of the resultôÙing perôÙseôÙcuôÙtion. Judas, the betrayôÙer of Jesus, ãremains the most conôÙtroôÙverôÙsial figôÙure in all of ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty,ã though quesôÙtions about his motiôÙvaôÙtions have gone withôÙout definôÙiôÙtive answers. We do know, howôÙevôÙer, that remorse evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly overôÙtook him, leadôÙing him to take his own life in AkelôÙdama, or the ãfield of bloodã ã and if you believe Dante, he now resides in the ninth cirôÙcle of Hell.
Trelawny gives the title of most underôÙratôÙed to the one whose skepôÙtiôÙcism about Jesusã return from death has guarôÙanôÙteed him his own eterôÙnal life through the expresôÙsion ãdoubtôÙing Thomas.ã (As with Peter and Judas, his idenôÙtiôÙty was solidôÙiôÙfied by aô CarôÙavagôÙgio paintôÙing.) AccordôÙing to cerôÙtain stoôÙries, he also travôÙeled the farôÙthest of any of the disôÙciôÙples: far enough to folôÙlow existôÙing Roman spice routes and found the church of the Saint Thomas ChrisôÙtians in KerôÙala, India. The not-quite-as-wideôÙly known but nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless highôÙly imporôÙtant Andrew made travôÙels of his own, going to Scythia, and from there to Greece. After his evenôÙtuôÙal capôÙture and cruôÙciôÙfixôÙion, his holy relics were scatôÙtered far and wide: even to ScotôÙland, so the legôÙend has it, home of the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of St. Andrews. The St. Andrewsã Cross appears as the main design eleôÙment of ScotôÙlandãs nationôÙal flag, as well as a part of the Union Jack.
In these and othôÙer ways, the legaôÙcies of the disôÙciôÙples conôÙtinôÙue to manôÙiôÙfest in familôÙiar ways throughôÙout the WestôÙern (and, occaôÙsionôÙalôÙly, non-westôÙern) world. After telling the stoôÙries of the remainôÙing eight, from John to Bartholomew to Simon the Zealot, Trelawny conôÙsidôÙers the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty of a mnemonôÙic rhyme for their fates. Alas, he admits, ãIãm still tryôÙing to think of what goes with ãflayed alive by ArmeôÙniôÙans.ãô ã Being a disôÙciôÙple of Jesus turns out, for the most part, to have been a callôÙing with a very low surôÙvival rate indeed. But then, in earôÙly ChrisôÙtianôÙiôÙty, marôÙtyrôÙdom was a holy act, a demonôÙstraôÙtion of devoôÙtion in imiôÙtaôÙtion of the MesôÙsiôÙah himôÙself ã and an eleôÙment sure to make most any disôÙciôÙple biopic a grueôÙsome viewôÙing expeôÙriôÙence.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The Real StoôÙry of EastôÙer: How We Got from the First EastôÙer in the Bible to BunôÙnies, Eggs & ChocoôÙlate
The GnosôÙtic Gospels: An IntroôÙducôÙtion to the ForôÙbidôÙden TeachôÙings of Jesus
Why Real BibôÙliôÙcal Angels Are Creepy, BeastôÙly, and HardôÙly AngelôÙic
How Our DepicôÙtion of Jesus Changed Over 2,000 Years and What He May Have ActuôÙalôÙly Looked Like
How Many Lives Does God Take in the Bible: An InvesôÙtiôÙgaôÙtion into a SurôÙprisôÙingôÙly High Body Count
What Makes Caravaggioãs The TakôÙing of Christ a TimeôÙless, Great PaintôÙing?
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: How John Coltrane Introduced the World to His Radical Sound with His Recording of ãMy Favorite Thingsã (1961)
description: John Coltrane released ãmore sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcant worksã than his 1960 ãMy Favorite Things,ã says Robin WashôÙingôÙton in a PRX docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary on the
body:
John Coltrane released ãmore sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcant worksã than his 1960 ãMy Favorite Things,ã says Robin WashôÙingôÙton in a PRX docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary on the clasôÙsic reworkôÙing of Rodgers and Hammersteinãs BroadôÙway hit. ãA Love Supremeã is often citôÙed as the zenith of the saxophonistãs career. ãBut if you tried to explain that song to an averôÙage lisôÙtenôÙer, you would lose them. [ãMy Favorite Thingsã] is a definôÙiôÙtive work that everyôÙone knows, and anyôÙone can lisôÙten to, and the fasôÙciôÙnatôÙing stoôÙry of its evoôÙluôÙtion is someôÙthing everyôÙone can share and enjoy.ã The song is accesôÙsiôÙble, a comôÙmerôÙcialôÙly sucôÙcessôÙful hit, and it is also an experôÙiôÙmenôÙtal masôÙterôÙpiece.
Indeed, ãMy Favorite Thingsã may be the perôÙfect introôÙducôÙtion to Coltraneãs experôÙiôÙmenôÙtalôÙism. After the dizzyôÙing chord changes of 1959ãs ãGiant Steps,ã this 14-minute, two-chord excurôÙsion patôÙterned on the ragas of Ravi Shankar announced Coltraneãs move into the modal forms he refined until his death in 1967, as well as his embrace of the sopraôÙno saxôÙoôÙphone and his new quarôÙtet. It became ãColtraneãs most requestôÙed tune,ã says Ed WheelôÙer in The World AccordôÙing to John Coltrane, ãand a bridge to a broad pubôÙlic audiôÙence.ã
Coltraneãs take is also mesôÙmerôÙizôÙing, trance-inducôÙing, ãoften comôÙpared to a whirling dervish,ã notes the PolyôÙphonôÙic video above, a refôÙerôÙence to the Sufi medôÙiôÙtaôÙtion techôÙnique of spinôÙning in a cirôÙcle. Itãs an unlikeôÙly song choice for the exerôÙcise, which makes it all the more fasôÙciôÙnatôÙing. The Sound of Music, Rodgers and Hammersteinãs final BroadôÙway colôÙlabôÙoôÙraôÙtion, was an ãinstant clasôÙsic,ã and everyôÙone whoãd seen it walked away humôÙming the tune to ãMy Favorite Things.ã By 1960, it had become a stanôÙdard, with sevôÙerôÙal covôÙer verôÙsions released by Leslie Uggams, The Pete King Chorale, the Hi-Loãs, and the NorôÙman Luboff Choir.


	

HunôÙdreds more covôÙers would folôÙlow. None of them soundôÙed like Coltraneãs. The modal formãin which musiôÙcians improôÙvise in difôÙferôÙent kinds of scales over simôÙpliôÙfied chord structuresãcreated the ãopen freeôÙdomã in music explored on Miles Davisã pathôÙbreakôÙing Kind of Blue, on which Coltrane played tenor sax. (It was Davis who bought Coltrane his first sopraôÙno sax that year.) Coltraneãs use of modal form in adapôÙtaôÙtions of popôÙuôÙlar stanôÙdards like ãMy Favorite Thingsã and George Gershwinãs ãSumôÙmerôÙtimeã from PorôÙgy and Bessô was an explicôÙit stratôÙeôÙgy to court a wider pubôÙlic, using the familôÙiar to oriôÙent his lisôÙtenôÙers to the new.
The video essay brings in the experôÙtise of musiôÙcian, comôÙposôÙer, and YouTuôÙber Adam Neely, who explains what makes Rogers and Hammersteinãs clasôÙsic unique among show tunes, and why it appealed to Coltrane as the cenôÙterôÙpiece of the 1961 album of the same name. The songãs unusuôÙal form and strucôÙture allow the same melody to be played over both major and minor chords. Coltraneãs modôÙiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion of the song reduces it to the two tonôÙics, E major and E minor, over which he and the band solo, introôÙducôÙing a shiftôÙing tonalôÙiôÙty and mood to the melody with each chord change.


	

Neely goes into greater depth, but itãs overôÙall an accesôÙsiôÙble explaôÙnaôÙtion of Coltraneãs very accesôÙsiôÙble, yet verôÙtigôÙiôÙnousôÙly deep, ãMy Favorite Things.ã Maybe only one quesôÙtion remains. Coltraneãs renôÙdiôÙtion came out four years before Julie Andrewsã iconôÙic perôÙforôÙmance in the film adapôÙtaôÙtion of The Sound of Music, evokôÙing the obviôÙous quesôÙtion,ã says WashôÙingôÙton: ãDid he influôÙence her?ã
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Jazz DeconôÙstructôÙed: What Makes John Coltraneãs ãGiant Stepsã So GroundôÙbreakôÙing and RadôÙiôÙcal?
John Coltrane Draws a PicôÙture IllusôÙtratôÙing the MathôÙeôÙmatôÙics of Music
DisôÙcovôÙer the Church of St. John Coltrane, FoundôÙed on the Divine Music ofô A Love Supreme
Behold John Coltraneãs HandôÙwritôÙten OutôÙline for His MasôÙterôÙpieceô A Love Supreme
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: When the Nobel Prize Committee Rejected The Lord of the Rings: Tolkien ãHas Not Measured Up to Storytelling of the Highest Qualityã (1961)
description: When J.R.R. Tolkienãsô Lord of the Ringsô books appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met withô veryô mixed reviews, an unsurôÙprisôÙing recepôÙtion givôÙ
body:
When J.R.R. Tolkienãsô Lord of the Ringsô books appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met withô veryô mixed reviews, an unsurôÙprisôÙing recepôÙtion givôÙen that nothôÙing like them had been writôÙten for adult readôÙers since Edmund Spenserãs epic 16th cenôÙtuôÙry EngôÙlish poemô The Faerie Queene, perôÙhaps. At least, this was the conôÙtention of reviewôÙer Richard HughôÙes, who went on to write that ãfor width of imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion,ãô The Lord of the Ringsô ãalmost begôÙgars parôÙalôÙlel.ã
ScotôÙtish writer NaoôÙmi MitchiôÙson did find a comôÙparôÙiôÙson: to Sir Thomas MalôÙoôÙry, author of the 15th cenôÙtuôÙryô Le Morte dãArthur ã hardôÙly misôÙplaced, givôÙen Tolkienãs day job as an Oxford don of EngôÙlish litôÙerôÙaôÙture, but not the sort of thing that passed for conôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary writôÙing in the 1950s, notwithôÙstandôÙing the seriôÙous appreôÙciôÙaôÙtion of writôÙers like W.H. Auden for Tolkienãs trilôÙoôÙgy. ãNo preôÙviôÙous writer,ã the poet remarked in aô New York Times review, ãhas, to my knowlôÙedge, creôÙatôÙed an imagôÙiôÙnary world and a feigned hisôÙtoôÙry in such detail.ã
Auden did find fault with Tolkienãs poetôÙry, a fact upon which critôÙic Edmund WilôÙson seized in his scathing 1956 Lord of the Ringsô review. ãMr. Auden is apparôÙentôÙly quite insenôÙsiôÙtive ã through lack of interôÙest in the othôÙer departôÙment,ã wrote WilôÙson, ãto the fact that Tolkienãs prose is just as bad. Prose and verse are on the same levôÙel of proôÙfesôÙsoôÙrôÙiôÙal amaôÙteurôÙishôÙness.ã Five years latôÙer, the Nobel prize jury would make the same judgeôÙment when they excludôÙed Tolkienãs books from conôÙsidôÙerôÙaôÙtion. Tolkienãs prose, wrote jury memôÙber Anders ûsterôÙling, ãhas not in any way meaôÙsured up to stoôÙryôÙtelling of the highôÙest qualôÙiôÙty.ã
The note was disôÙcovôÙered recentôÙly by Swedish jourôÙnalôÙist Andreas EkstrûÑm, who delved into the Nobel archive for 1961 and found that ãthe jury passed over names includôÙing Lawrence DurôÙrell, Robert Frost, GraôÙham Greene, E.M. Forster, and Tolkien to come up with their evenôÙtuôÙal winôÙner, YugoslaôÙvian writer Ivo Andriá,ã as AliôÙson Flood reports atô The Guardian. (The Nobel archives are sealed until 50 years after the year the award is givôÙen.) EkstrûÑm has been readôÙing through the archives ãfor the past five years or so,ã he says, ãand this was the first time I have seen Tolkienãs name among the sugôÙgestôÙed canôÙdiôÙdates.ã His name appeared on the list chiefly through the machiôÙnaôÙtions of his closôÙest friend and chief supôÙportôÙer, C.S. Lewis.
Lewis, ãalso of Oxford,ã WilôÙson sneered, ãis able to top them allã in praise of Tolkienãs books. From the first appearôÙance of his MidôÙdle Earth fanôÙtaôÙsy inô The HobôÙbit,ô Lewis promised to ãdo all in my powôÙer to secure for Tolkienãs great book the recogôÙniôÙtion it deserves,ã as he wrote in a 1953 letôÙter to British pubôÙlishôÙer StanôÙley Unwin. In what might be conôÙsidôÙered an unethôÙiôÙcal proôÙmoôÙtion of his friendãs work today, Lewis respondôÙed tireôÙlessôÙly to critôÙics of the trilôÙoôÙgy, going so far, after the pubôÙliôÙcaôÙtion ofô The Two TowôÙers, to pen an essay on the subôÙject titled ãThe DethroneôÙment of PowôÙer.ã Here, Lewis explains the proôÙlix qualôÙiôÙty of Tolkienãs prose ã that which critôÙics called ãtediousã ã as a narôÙraôÙtive necesôÙsiôÙty: ãI do not think he could have done it any othôÙer way.ã
Tolkienãs biggest fan also urged readôÙers to spend more time with the books and promised that the rewards would be great. In defense of the secôÙond work of the trilôÙoôÙgy, he conôÙcludôÙed, ãthe book is too origôÙiôÙnal and too opuôÙlent for any final judgôÙment on a first readôÙing. But we know at once that it has done things to us. We are not quite the same men. And though we must ration ourôÙselves in our rereadôÙings, I have litôÙtle doubt that the book will soon take its place among the indisôÙpensôÙables.ã And so has all of Tolkienãs work, becomôÙing the litôÙerôÙary stanôÙdard by which high fanôÙtaôÙsy is meaôÙsured, with or withôÙout a Nobel prize.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2021.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
J.R.R. Tolkien Snubs a GerôÙman PubôÙlishôÙer AskôÙing for Proof of His ãAryan Descentã (1938)
110 DrawôÙings and PaintôÙings by J.R.R. Tolkien: Of MidôÙdle-Earth and Beyond
J.R.R. Tolkien Expressed a ãHeartôÙfelt Loathingã for Walt DisôÙney and Refused to Let DisôÙney StuôÙdios Adapt His Work
DisôÙcovôÙer J.R.R. Tolkienãs LitôÙtle-Known and Hand-IllusôÙtratôÙed Childrenãs Book, Mr. Bliss
When J.R.R. Tolkien Worked for theô Oxford EngôÙlish DicôÙtioôÙnaryô and ãLearned More ãÎ Than Any OthôÙer Equal PeriôÙod of My Lifeã (1919ã1920)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Creative Thought Is Essential: A Letter from Our Editor
description:
Dear readers,
In a collection of her published diary entries, Virgina Woolf wrote, ãThinking is my fighting.ã This sentiment was a prominent theme
body:
Dear readers,
In a collection of her published diary entries, Virgina Woolf wrote, ãThinking is my fighting.ã This sentiment was a prominent theme in her 1940 essay, Thoughts on Peace In an Air Raid, in which the British writer framed the importance of individual thought as a crucial antidote to war and the rise of fascism.
Iãve been thinking a lot about Woolfãs quote and its relevance today, largely connected to discourse on education. While literacy rates in the U.S. continue to plummet, a soaring number of individuals are adopting habits antithetical to thinkingãoffloading even the smallest of cognitive tasks onto Chat GPT, doomscrolling for hours on social media, and numbing the mind with addictive ãbrain rot.ã Critical thinking is being traded for passive acceptance and the death of curiosityãall in the name of efficiency and capital innovation. Our minds have become the new commodity.
In response to this, Iãve seen a clear cultural shift toward self-education. I, too, have been influenced by this new wave of autodidactic learningãpersonal curricula, digital detoxes, reading challenges, etc. While these are certainly more examples of internet trends worming their way into our algorithms, beneath them lies something deeper: a collective desire to reclaim our own thoughts.
At the same time, access to quality information and educational materials can be hard to find, whether those resources are locked behind the gates of inflated tuition costs, class fees, or online paywalls. For Colossal, accessibility has always been an ongoing commitment. Over the last 15 years and counting, every article, interview, and instructional aid on our site has remained completely free. Our team believes that the ability to learn about and enjoy art must be universally available.
ãIãve been using Colossal as my go-to source of art on the internet since 2012 when I was in high school. Itãs been a pleasure watching it grow and keep up with the breadth and depth of the work being done in the art world.ãJake F.
For years, members of this community have made us aware of how beneficial Colossal has been in their learning journeys. Educators of all ages have shared that theyãve introduced the website to their students, crafting complete lesson plans from our articles and glossary of art terms. I encountered Colossal for the first time in a classroom, and in another life where I taught elementary students art, it remained one of my most reliable tools.
Out of the classroom, learning persists, and weãve been elated to hear many stories about how our daily doses of art allow readers to uncover something new everyday, gain more knowledge about contemporary art, and discover impactful stories that bring about change, connection, and hope.
Now more than ever, creative thought must be nurtured, protected, and free from barriers. Our mission to do this can only continue with you. Today, only 1% of Colossal readers financially support the work we do. Like many independent publications, our members are the cornerstone of our existence and ability to continue sharing art with the world.
We encourage you to join the Colossal community. Now is the time to spread creative ideas, revive curiosity, and defend the ability to think and learn imaginatively.
And as we celebrate teacher appreciation this month, weãd like to highlight our deeply discounted education membership, available to current students and educators (just shoot as a quick email and weãll get you set up). Thank you for reading, sharing, and learning with us.
Gratefully,Jackie AndresOnline editor, Colossal

title: Bespoke Glass Studio's Sculptures Challenge Traditional Conventions of Stained Glass
description: With a stained glass window, light filters through to illuminate narrative scenes or geometric patterns, but itãs primarily the window itself that
body:
With a stained glass window, light filters through to illuminate narrative scenes or geometric patterns, but itãs primarily the window itself that draws our attention. For Lesley Green of Bespoke Glass, these vibrant compositions certainly arenãt limited to these traditional apertures. ãOne of my personal obsessions is trying to convince people to hang glass on the wall instead of in the window, so you can really experience the pure color and texture of the glass,ã she tells Colossal.
Bespoke Glass creates a wide range of aesthetic and functional forms, conceived for both residential and commercial interiors. Some are designed to be screens or separators, such as behind a bar or between tables in a restaurant. Others are more sculptural, such as her three-dimensional sculptures that project onto the wall when the sun shines through them, interacting with local shadows. This display method also highlights the inherent textures of the glass itself, from waves to ridges to mottled patterns.

Using a hand-cut, traditional copper foil method of creating the stained glass, Green has also innovated some studio-developed techniques to produce three-dimensional objects. ãCraftsmanship is extremely important to me as well,ã she says. ãPrecise cuts and especially smooth solder lines are part of everything my studio produces.ã
All pieces are available to be commissioned in custom colorways, and Green is also working on larger-scale versions of the sculptural works, plus multiple-piece collections designed to installed on the wall. See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Bespoke Glass Studio's Sculptures Challenge Traditional Conventions of Stained Glass

title: Markus Brunetti's Monumental Photos Venerate European Ecclesiastical Landmarks
description: For a little more than two decades, Bavarian photographer Markus Brunetti has scoured Europe for its most impressive basilicas, monasteries, duomi
body:
For a little more than two decades, Bavarian photographer Markus Brunetti has scoured Europe for its most impressive basilicas, monasteries, duomi, and other striking ecclesiastical landmarks. Working closely with collaborator Betty SchûÑner, with whom he travels around the continent in a firetruck that has been converted to a photo lab, the pair snap thousands of images of each structure in meter-by-meter detail, often over the course of several years.
Through a meticulous editing process that includes layering and arranging each shot into composite images, Brunetti creates precise, high-resolution views of the facades that we never experience in real life. Perspective is skewed so that the ornate temples and cathedralsã entrances are perfectly straight. Rather than the oblique view we usually getãthink of how tall structures look when viewed from the street, with their base appearing wider and the top growing gradually narrowerãweãre confronted with a striking one-point perspective.
ãSantiago de Compostela, Catedralã (2009-2024), archival pigment print, image 83 3/4 x 54 1/4 inches
Brunettiãs current solo exhibition, Facades IV at Yossi Milo, highlights a selection of the artistãs recent portraits, several of which were completed in the last couple of years. ãRoma, Basilica di San Pietro,ã for example, was initiated in 2007. ãBrunetti and SchûÑner returned to St. Peterãs Basilica seven times over nineteen years,ã the gallery says. ãWith each survey, they grew closer to realizing this grand imageãa particular challenge given that it is one of the largest and most visited churches in the world.ã
Printed at an impressively large scaleãup to seven-and-a-half feet tallãthe photos venerate these buildings, many of which are centuries old. ãThe result exceeds the possibilities of any single photograph, even at the highest possible resolution, creating works that stand as monuments in and of themselves,ã the gallery says.
Facades IV continues through June 20 in New York City.
ãRoma, Basilica di San Pietroã (2007-2026), archival pigment print, image 58 1/4 x 58 1/4 inches
ãBucuresti, Templul Coralã (2018-2019), archival pigment print, image 66 1/8 x 54 5/16 inches
ãMolfetta, Duomo di San Corradoã (2011-2026), archival pigment print, image 66 1/8 x 54 5/16 inches 
ãNoyon, Cathûˋdral Notre-Dameã (2018-2026), archival pigment print, image  83 3/4 x 54 1/4 inches
ãBadia Fiesolana, Fiesoleã (2022-2025), archival pigment print, image 66 1/8 x 54 5/16 inches
ãLãAquila, Basilica di San Bernardinoã (2014-2026), archival pigment print, image 58 1/4 x 58 1/4 inches
ãVenezia, Il Redentoreã (2012-2023), archival pigment print, image 83 3/4 x 54 1/4 inches
